title: Sponsoring Someone With More Anger Than You Can Hold
dek: When you sit across from a fury that threatens to swallow the room, your body knows before your mind does. Here is how to hold the space without letting your own nervous system catch fire.
bodyMarkdown:
## Sitting Across From a Storm


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.073Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It’s 7:43 pm, the room dimming as the last gold of a late spring day slips through the cracked blinds. You sit across from her, the woman who’s just unloaded a torrent of fury you weren’t prepared to carry. Her shoulders, thick and tense, rise sharply with each breath; the levator scapulae hitch near her neck, a silent scream in muscle and fascia. You can smell it - raw earth, sweat, a faint trace of peppermint oil she rubbed on moments ago to “calm down.” But calm isn’t here. What’s here is a living presence, a storm of anger that pulses through the air between you, a palpable heat that your diaphragm tightens against even as you try to sit still.

This is not merely her problem. It’s yours now, too. Your masseter clenches involuntarily; your trapezius knots, a slow coil tightening from shoulder blade to neck. You feel your own body respond - not because you’re broken or weak, but because this is biology in motion. Anger is a messenger, not a moral failure or a character flaw. It’s a message encoded in muscle tension, in every quickened heartbeat and shallow breath.

For centuries, women like you were taught that fury is ugly, dangerous, something to be hidden or silenced. But here, in this charged room, you’re learning a different truth. The anger spilling from her body is as real and urgent as your own need to breathe deeply and unbind your ribs. As Karla McLaren teaches, anger carries essential information - about boundaries, injustice, survival. It’s not a problem to be fixed but a signal to be received, even if it overwhelms you.

You are sponsoring this storm, not by fixing it, but by holding the space where her fury can live without shame. This is the work: to recognize that your own nervous system will react, will coil, will want to shut down or explode in turn. But you do not have to collapse under the weight. You learn to track the cascade of sensations in your body - the rapid pulse in your carotid artery, the subtle ache behind your eyes, the way your psoas resists the urge to flee - because the body remembers. This is not pathology; this is biology. And in this biology, there is a somatic conversation waiting to be heard.

## The Somatic Reality of Holding Space

You know that moment when someone across from you is seething, a volcanic storm of fury rolling just beneath their skin, and you find yourself the reluctant vessel for all that energy? It’s not abstract. It’s not metaphor. The body knows. In my own body, holding space for another’s massive anger is a choreography of muscles, breath, and nervous system shifts that never lie.

First, the masseter clamps tight, as if bracing for impact. The jaw clenches, teeth grinding invisibly, the fascia around the mandible stiffening like a taut wire. I feel the heaviness of my head anchoring forward, the subtle forward jut of the chin as if to protect a vulnerable throat. The levator scapulae in my neck contracts - those muscles that cradle the skull’s base - pulling upward and inward with a quiet insistence, bracing against the tension radiating from the other person’s voice and presence.

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that “the body keeps the score.” Here, it’s clear: my diaphragm freezes halfway, caught between the urge to exhale fully and the necessity to hold the breath steady. The psoas, that deep core muscle often called the muscle of the soul, tightens reflexively, anchoring me to the earth but also creating a subtle compression around the kidneys, the seat of our primal survival instincts. It’s as if my body is simultaneously a shield and a container, bracing to absorb the raw energy without shattering.

Peter Levine’s work on somatic experiencing comes alive in this encounter. The nervous system is not separate - it is a dance of co-regulation or dysregulation. When I meet another’s fury, the autonomic nervous system flickers between sympathetic alarm and parasympathetic attempt at soothing. If I am unprepared, the trapezius muscles flare up, knots forming along the upper back and shoulders, a physical echo of hypervigilance. The skin prickles; sweat beads at the nape of the neck. My heartbeat accelerates, not in my own anger, but in response to the other’s storm.

What’s remarkable - and sometimes terrifying - is how easily the nervous systems entwine. If I am calm, grounded, breathing deeply, my parasympathetic nervous system can help guide theirs back from the edge. But if my own psoas is clenched, my diaphragm shallow, my masseter locked tight, I risk becoming a mirror of their dysregulation rather than a container for it. The distinction matters. This is not pathology, this is biology.

In these moments, herbs like [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) become allies - not magic cures, but botanical support to ease the diaphragm’s tension and soften the jaw’s grip. The body remembers, the jaw remembers. To hold space is to hold breathing room for not just their fury, but your own somatic wisdom to stay present without becoming undone.

## When Their Anger Triggers Your Own

It’s late afternoon, the light outside dull and gray, the kind of sky that presses down just enough to make your breath feel heavier. You’re sitting across from someone whose anger isn’t just loud - it’s a low, rumbling growl that shakes the air between you. Their fury is a wave crashing into the shore of your own buried rage, the one you’ve been carrying like a secret knot in your solar plexus, tight and unyielding.

You feel it first in your throat, a catch that tightens the cricothyroid muscle, making your voice shallow and brittle. Then comes the heat, rising from your diaphragm up through the levator scapulae, setting your neck and shoulders on fire. Your masseter clenches reflexively, teeth grinding, a sharp ache pulsing like a warning in your jaw’s fascia. This is no polite disagreement. It’s a visceral collision - a meeting of two fires that cannot be extinguished with silence.

Karla McLaren calls anger a genius, and here it is, its brilliance undeniable. Anger doesn’t apologize. It doesn’t soften itself to make others comfortable. It demands boundaries, fierce and clear, like a line drawn in the earth with a burning stick. When their anger breaks through your defenses, it stabs at the places you’ve tried to numb or smooth over: the long-held “should not” and “must not” that have kept your own fury locked behind a door labeled “Not Now.” Suddenly, that door rattles; your own anger stirs, ready to claim its space.

You might notice your psoas tightening under your ribs, the core muscle bracing for fight or flight even if your mind tries to reason the situation away. The body remembers before you do; it is the custodian of your histories, the silent chronicler of every time you swallowed down rage so deep it settled like sediment in your lungs. Now, it rises, uninvited but unavoidable, a tidal pulse that demands to be seen and honored.

This is not pathology. This is biology. Your body is speaking in the language of muscle and fascia, of breath that suddenly shortens and thickens. The anger you were taught to hide is answering the call, not as a flaw but as a fierce protector. It is saying: “This boundary is mine.” When their anger triggers yours, it’s a sacred moment - a raw, unfiltered communication from body to body, a chance to listen to the messages written in tension and heat.

You don’t have to answer with more fury, but you can’t pretend the fire isn’t there. It’s a signal, a boundary, a call to reclaim your voice that was once told to stay silent. And if you listen closely, you’ll hear the wisdom beneath the roar.

## The Biology of Boundaries

You’ve probably heard about boundaries as something mental - lines you draw with words, firm “no’s” you string together to keep people out. But before your mind even forms those sentences, your body is already at work, setting its own borders. Boundaries live in your fascia, the dense, sticky web of connective tissue that wraps every muscle, organ, and nerve like a second skin. It’s not just a passive sheath; it’s a dynamic, responsive terrain that registers threat and safety with a precision no thought can match.

When you feel your anger rise - maybe the familiar tightness in your masseter, the jaw muscle that clenches before your words do - you’re not just “being emotional.” Your fascia is bracing, thickening, contracting. It pulls taut beneath your skin, tightening the trapezius at the base of your neck, stiffening the levator scapulae that lifts your shoulders close to your ears. This is fascia’s way of drawing a line in the sand, tightening your edges so no one crosses into the vulnerable territories of your body and psyche. It’s biology, not drama.

Resmaa Menakem, in his work on somatic abolitionism, teaches that healing trauma - racial or otherwise - begins in the body. He insists that settling the body is the first radical act of boundary-setting. Not because you’re forcing your mind to say “stop,” but because you’re teaching your nervous system what safety feels like. You ground yourself by tuning into the slow, solid weight of your feet on the floor, the subtle press of your sit bones against a chair, the rhythmic rise and fall of your diaphragm. This isn’t a metaphor; it’s a conversation with your fascia and muscles, telling them they can relax, that the threat is not here.

You can almost smell the difference in your body’s chemistry when you do this - like the damp earth after rain, cool and alive. The fascia loosens, the psoas muscles - those deep core stabilizers that coil around your spine - soften their grip. Your breath becomes a gentle tide, washing tension away. In this state, your boundaries aren’t brittle walls but flexible membranes that protect without shutting down. They ripple and bend with the world, but they don’t break.

This is what teachers like Karla McLaren and Bessel van der Kolk remind us: anger isn’t a failure of control. It’s the body’s urgent signal that boundaries have been breached. When your fascia stiffens, it’s your body saying, “I’m here. I’m claiming this space.” Grounding yourself is the act of listening back, of saying, “I see you. I won’t let you be invaded.” In this way, the biology of boundaries is a matter of survival, a daily practice of settling the body so your fury doesn’t have to carry the weight of unspoken defenses.

## Herbal and Somatic Support

When fury curls in your belly like a live wire, the body’s nervous system is screaming for relief - not sedation, not denial, but deep, respectful care. Herbal allies have been walking this path with us for centuries, their medicine rooted in the flesh and bone of human experience. Take Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca), for instance. This herb tastes like dark honey with a bitter edge, a green bitterness that slides along your tongue and lingers in the back of your throat. Its scent is faintly minty, a whisper of wild meadow herbs, sharp and grounding. Motherwort’s magic lies in its affinity for the heart and nervous system; it soothes the tightness in your chest, loosening the masseter muscle around your jaw where anger often lodges. The diaphragm, too, finds relief - Motherwort encourages a slow, deep breath, opening the chest as if the body remembers how to expand after years of holding in rage.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is another ally, its name a nod to the shape of its tiny blue flowers, shaped like medieval helmets. Its taste is mildly bitter with a subtle sweetness, a clean, earthy flavor that reminds you of damp forest floor after rain. The aroma is faint but unmistakably fresh, like crushed green leaves. Skullcap’s action is more subtle but no less profound. It calms the levator scapulae - the muscle that runs from your neck to your shoulder blade, often knotted and screaming in moments of frustration. This herb is a gentle sedative for the nervous system, quieting the fire in your sympathetic nervous system without dulling your awareness. It’s the breath in the storm, the quiet pulse under chaos.

Milky Oats (Avena sativa) stands out for its nurturing, tonic qualities, especially when anger leaves you raw and depleted. The taste is mildly sweet and grassy, like freshly cut oat straw warmed by the sun. Its scent is soft and comforting, reminiscent of old barns and golden fields. Milky Oats feed the nervous system’s resilience, mending the fascia that holds tension in the back and neck, encouraging the trapezius muscles to soften and release. You might notice a subtle warmth spreading across your shoulders and down your spine, a gentle unfolding as if your body remembers how to relax after holding itself tight for too long.

Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) offers a wisdom that aligns with these Western herbs, framing anger as a stagnation of Liver Qi, the life force that moves through your body like a river. When Liver Qi stalls, the body tightens. Herbs like Bupleurum (Chai Hu) and formulations such as Xiao Yao San are designed to move this Qi gently, easing the tension in your psoas muscle - the deep core muscle that braces your lower spine and pelvis. This muscle is notorious for clenching in moments of fury, anchoring your anger in your very structure. TCM reminds us that the body is a landscape where emotions flow, and when that flow stops, it’s the body that signals, sometimes with clenched fists or a racing heart.

These herbs don’t erase anger - they honor it. They support the nervous system’s ability to hold fire without burning down the house. You can feel the difference in your body’s texture: the subtle softening of muscle fibers, the slow return of your breath to a natural rhythm, the quieting of the electrical hum under your skin. Try Motherwort tincture like this one [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20), taken in small doses when your jaw locks or your chest feels like a drum. Respect the plants as you would your own anger - neither enemy nor weakness, but messenger and medicine.

## The Weight of the Unspoken

When you sit across from someone whose anger is a roaring fire, there is a physical toll to the words you swallow. The body remembers every syllable you bite back, every truth you decide is too dangerous to speak in the face of their storm. This unspoken weight doesn’t just vanish; it settles into the architecture of your throat and chest, a heavy, leaden presence that demands to be felt.

You feel it first in the cricothyroid muscle, the delicate band that controls the pitch of your voice. It tightens, a reflexive bracing against the urge to shout, to defend, to say, “Enough.” The vocal cords themselves feel thick, coated in a silence that tastes like ash. This is the somatic reality of holding space when the space demands your own erasure. The jaw remembers, the masseter clenching so hard it sends a dull ache radiating up into your temples. You are not just listening; you are actively suppressing the biological imperative to respond.

The chest, too, bears the brunt of this unspoken weight. The intercostal muscles between your ribs stiffen, turning your ribcage into a rigid cage rather than a flexible bellows. Your breath becomes shallow, trapped in the upper chest, unable to reach the diaphragm. It’s as if your body is trying to make itself smaller, to take up less space in a room already overflowing with someone else’s fury. The heart beats against this constriction, a frantic bird against a closed window, signaling a distress that your mind might try to ignore.

This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows that the unspoken words are a form of self-abandonment, a betrayal of your own boundaries. The fascia around your heart and lungs thickens, a protective measure against the onslaught of their anger and the internal pressure of your own silence. You might feel a sudden, sharp pain between your shoulder blades, the rhomboids knotting as they try to hold you upright when you want to collapse.

To hold space for someone else’s anger without losing yourself requires acknowledging this weight. It means noticing the tightness in your throat and the shallow breath in your chest, and choosing to breathe into those spaces anyway. It means recognizing that the words you swallow are not gone; they are waiting in the muscle and fascia, demanding to be honored. The body remembers, and eventually, it will ask for those words back.

## Embodied Practice: The Grounding Drop

You’re standing too close to someone’s fury, their anger roaring like a storm you didn’t call up. The air thickens with tension, a pressure that settles heavy in your trapezius and levator scapulae, the muscles at the back and side of your neck knotting like tangled wires. Your breath clips short, the diaphragm spasming just beneath your ribs, as if trying to hold you hostage in that moment. This is when you need the Grounding Drop.

Begin by planting your feet firmly on the floor. Feel the soles press into the cool hardness beneath you - the grain of the wood, the chill of tile, the slight give of carpet fibers. Your feet are your anchors, the connection to the earth that never wavers, even when voices rise and tempers flare. Notice the subtle spread of your toes, the way your arches curve, the weight settling first into the balls of your feet, then rolling back to your heels. This is your base.

Now, draw your attention to the muscles around your jaw - the masseter, clenched tight like a clenched fist you can’t see. Without forcing it, allow a slow, deliberate unclenching. Let your lower jaw drop just enough to create space, the soft tissue beneath your chin loosening like a sigh. This small release sends a message through your nervous system: you are not in danger, not the source of this anger.

Inhale deeply, filling the belly so your diaphragm expands downward, pushing the abdominal muscles outward. The breath should be slow, swollen, and full-bodied, like the tide coming in. Hold it for a count of three, feeling the stretch in your ribs and the subtle lift of your chest. Then exhale all the way out through your nose, slow and steady, as if you’re blowing across the surface of a still pond. With this exhale, imagine the tension pooling in your shoulders and neck draining away, melting out through your fingertips.

Repeat this cycle - rooting into your feet, unclenching your jaw, breathing deep - until the storm outside you feels less like a tempest and more like wind passing through the trees. This is not about controlling them or your environment; this is about reclaiming your own body’s calm. The Grounding Drop is a reclaiming of your somatic space, a subtle but potent act of self-preservation when fury encroaches. The body remembers these small acts. The body knows how to settle.

## FAQ

You feel it before the words land - the tightening of your trapezius, the quick hitch in your breath as someone else’s fury makes itself known. The room gets hotter, or colder, the light tilts sideways, and suddenly your masseter clenches, your jaw remembers every time you swallowed down rage as a kid. It’s a visceral experience; anger isn’t just in the air, it’s in your body, and it wants something from you. So what do you do when you’re caught in the crossfire of someone else’s storm? When their heat threatens to consume you whole, and your first impulse is to flee?

**Q: What if I feel like I’m being consumed by someone else’s anger?**  
A: Your body is your first and truest ally here. That tightening in your chest, the shallow breath stuck under your diaphragm, the sudden urge to clench your fists - these are signals, not weaknesses. The anger you’re witnessing is theirs, not yours, even if your nervous system is firing off like a live wire in the rain. Notice where you hold the tension: is it your levator scapulae pinching behind your neck, or your psoas twisting in your belly? Naming it, feeling it, tracing it with your fingers or a slow, deliberate stretch can ground you. This is not about swallowing their fire or matching it with your own. It’s about respecting the boundary between their rage and your nervous system’s fragile architecture. Herbs like chamomile or skullcap can help soothe the frayed edges of your nerves afterward, but first, honor the body’s wisdom - it knows how to protect you.

**Q: When I want to run away, how can I stay present without losing myself?**  
A: The impulse to run is a primal survival mechanism, and there’s nothing wrong with it. Sometimes, stepping back across the room or even out the door is exactly what your diaphragm and nervous system demand. But if you want to stay, try this: ground your feet firmly into the floor, feel the weight of your sit bones on the chair, and soften the masseter - loosen your jaw by gently opening your mouth an inch or two. Slow your exhale, counting it out longer than your inhale. This shifts your parasympathetic nervous system into gear, letting the anger in the room pass over you rather than through you. You don’t have to absorb or fix their rage - you can witness it without drowning in it.

**Q: How do I know when it’s time to step back completely?**  
A: Trust your body’s red flags. They come as tightness in your trapezius that won’t ease, or a sudden pounding in your temples that no amount of breath can quell. Maybe it’s the cold sweat on your neck or a sinking pit in your stomach. These aren’t signs of weakness; they are your biology shouting, “Enough.” Stepping back can mean leaving the room, ending the conversation, or setting firmer boundaries. It’s an act of self-preservation, not abandonment. Remember, the body remembers every moment you’ve endured fury without protection. Listening to these somatic cues is how you rewrite your story - one where your nervous system can settle, and your anger is honored, not hijacked.
